title: Maharashtrian Bharli Vangi

ingredients.name: small brinjal (small green or purple variety), about 8–10 pieces

subs.name: 4 tbsp desiccated coconut soaked in 2 tbsp warm water; dry roast briefly before using
same quantity roasted cashew pieces; richer but works well
2 tbsp lemon juice added at the end, off heat; the sourness will be brighter

steps.text: Dry-roast the onion slices in a dry pan on medium heat, stirring, until they turn deep brown and start to dry out — about 7 minutes. Add garlic and ginger in the last minute and continue roasting. Cool slightly and grind with tomatoes to a coarse wet paste in a mixie.

In the same pan dry-roast the sesame seeds until golden (1 minute), then add coconut and roast until the coconut turns pale golden and fragrant (2–3 minutes). Combine roasted sesame, coconut and ground peanuts in a bowl. Add red chilli powder, coriander powder, half the turmeric, garam masala, tamarind, jaggery and salt. Mix into a moist, cohesive stuffing.

Wash the brinjals and make two deep cuts crosswise from the base almost to the stem, keeping the brinjal intact. Gently press the stuffing into the cuts of each brinjal, filling them generously. Reserve any leftover stuffing for the gravy.

Heat oil in a wide kadai on medium. Add mustard seeds; when they pop add curry leaves and the remaining turmeric. Gently nestle the stuffed brinjals in a single layer. Pour in the ground onion-tomato paste around the brinjals and sauté the masala for 2–3 minutes until the raw smell goes.

Add any leftover stuffing to the gravy along with 0.75 cup water. Stir gently around the brinjals without disturbing them. Cover tightly and cook on low-medium for 18–20 minutes, turning the brinjals very carefully once at the halfway mark, until they are completely tender when pierced with a knife and the gravy has thickened.

Taste the gravy — it should be well balanced between the tamarind sourness, jaggery sweetness and chilli heat. Adjust any element. Scatter coriander leaves and serve hot with jowar or wheat bhakri, or with steamed rice.

substitution_notes: coconut → 4 tbsp desiccated coconut soaked in 2 tbsp warm water; dry roast briefly before using; peanut → same quantity roasted cashew pieces; richer but works well; tamarind → 2 tbsp lemon juice added at the end, off heat; the sourness will be brighter
